title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 6 (Fragment 1)
text:
The Magicians of Caprona

Chapter 6

Tonino read half the night. With all the grown-ups hard at work in the Scriptorium, there was no one to tell him to go to bed. Corinna tried, when she had finished her homework, but Tonino was too deep in the book even to hear her. And Corinna went respectfully away, thinking that, as the book had come from Uncle Umberto, it was probably very learned.

It was not in the least learned. It was the most gripping story Tonino had ever read. It started with the boy, Giorgio, going along a mysterious alleyway near the docks on his way home from school. There was a peeling blue house at the end of the alley and, just as Giorgio passed it, a scrap of paper fluttered from one of its windows. It contained a mysterious message, which led Giorgio at once into a set of adventures with the enemies of his country. Each one was more exciting than the last.

Well after midnight, when Giorgio was holding a pass single-handed against the enemy, Tonino happened to hear his father and mother coming to bed. He was forced to leave Giorgio lying wounded and dive into bed himself. All night he dreamed of notes fluttering from the windows of peeling blue houses, of Giorgio—who was sometimes Tonino himself and sometimes Paolo—and of villainous enemies—most of whom seemed to have red beards and black hair, like Guido Petrocchi—and, as the sun rose, he was too excited to stay asleep. He woke up and went on reading.

When the rest of the Casa Montana began to stir, Tonino had finished the book. Giorgio had saved his country. Tonino was quivering with excitement and exhaustion. He wished the book was twice as long. If it had not been time to get up, he would have gone straight back to the beginning and started reading the book again.

And the beauty of it, he thought, eating breakfast without noticing, was that Giorgio had saved his country, not only single-handed, but without a spell coming into it anywhere. If Tonino was going to save Caprona, that was the way he would like to do it.

Around Tonino, everyone else was complaining and Lucia was sulking. The washing-up spell was still about in the kitchen. Every cup and plate was covered with a thin layer of orange spaghetti grease, and the butter tasted of soap.

“What did she use , in Heaven’s name?” groaned Uncle Lorenzo. “This coffee tastes of tomato.”

“Her own words to the Angel of Caprona ,” Aunt Maria said, and shuddered as she picked up her greasy cup.

“Lucia, you fool!” said Rinaldo. “That’s the strongest tune there is.”

“All right, all right. Stop going on at me. I’m sorry!” Lucia said angrily.

“So are the rest of us, unfortunately,” sighed Uncle Lorenzo.

If only I could be like Giorgio, Tonino thought, as he got up from the table. I suppose what I should have to do is to find the words to the Angel . He went to school without seeing anything on the way, wondering how he could manage to do that, when the rest of his family had failed. He was realistic enough to know that he was simply not good enough at spells to make up the words in the ordinary way. It made him sigh heavily.

“Cheer up,” said Paolo, as they went into school.

“I’m all right,” Tonino said. He was surprised Paolo should think he was miserable. He was not miserable at all. He was wrapped in delightful dreams. Maybe I can do it by accident, he thought.

He sat in class composing strings of gibberish to the tune of the Angel , in hopes that some of it might be right. But that did not seem satisfactory, somehow. Then, in a lesson that was probably History—for he did not hear a word of it—it struck him, like a blinding light, what he had to do. He had to find the words, of course. The First Duke must have had them written down somewhere and lost the paper. Tonino was the boy whose mission it was to discover that lost paper. No nonsense about making up words, just straight detective work. And Tonino was positive that the book had been a clue. He must find a peeling blue house, and the paper with the words on would be somewhere near.

vocabulary.definition: An unusual and exciting, typically hazardous, experience or activity.
vocabulary.example: It contained a mysterious message, which led Giorgio at once into a set of adventures with the enemies of his country.

vocabulary.definition: Extremely bright or overpowering to the eye.
vocabulary.example: Then, in a lesson that was probably History—for he did not hear a word of it—it struck him, like a blinding light, what he had to do.

vocabulary.definition: A piece of evidence or information used in the detection of a crime or solving a mystery.
vocabulary.example: And Tonino was positive that the book had been a clue.

vocabulary.definition: Express dissatisfaction or annoyance about something.
vocabulary.example: Around Tonino, everyone else was complaining and Lucia was sulking.

vocabulary.definition: To create or put together, especially music or text.
vocabulary.example: He sat in class composing strings of gibberish to the tune of the Angel , in hopes that some of it might be right.

vocabulary.definition: A person, especially a member of the police, who investigates crimes and obtains evidence.
vocabulary.example: No nonsense about making up words, just straight detective work.

vocabulary.definition: A person who is actively opposed or hostile to someone or something.
vocabulary.example: It contained a mysterious message, which led Giorgio at once into a set of adventures with the enemies of his country.

vocabulary.definition: A state of extreme physical or mental fatigue.
vocabulary.example: Tonino was quivering with excitement and exhaustion.

vocabulary.definition: To wave or flap quickly and lightly.
vocabulary.example: There was a peeling blue house at the end of the alley and, just as Giorgio passed it, a scrap of paper fluttered from one of its windows.

vocabulary.definition: Speech or writing that is nonsense or unintelligible.
vocabulary.example: He sat in class composing strings of gibberish to the tune of the Angel , in hopes that some of it might be right.

vocabulary.definition: A thick oily substance used as a lubricant.
vocabulary.example: Every cup and plate was covered with a thin layer of orange spaghetti grease, and the butter tasted of soap.

vocabulary.definition: Very exciting and interesting.
vocabulary.example: It was the most gripping story Tonino had ever read.

vocabulary.definition: Make a deep, inarticulate sound in response to pain or despair.
vocabulary.example: ” groaned Uncle Lorenzo.

vocabulary.definition: Very unhappy or uncomfortable.
vocabulary.example: He was surprised Paolo should think he was miserable.

vocabulary.definition: An important task or duty assigned to someone.
vocabulary.example: Tonino was the boy whose mission it was to discover that lost paper.

vocabulary.definition: Difficult to understand, explain, or identify.
vocabulary.example: It started with the boy, Giorgio, going along a mysterious alleyway near the docks on his way home from school.

vocabulary.definition: Shaking slightly; trembling.
vocabulary.example: Tonino was quivering with excitement and exhaustion.

vocabulary.definition: Having a sensible and practical idea of what can be achieved.
vocabulary.example: He was realistic enough to know that he was simply not good enough at spells to make up the words in the ordinary way.

vocabulary.definition: Fulfilling expectations or needs.
vocabulary.example: But that did not seem satisfactory, somehow.

vocabulary.definition: A room in a monastery dedicated to the copying of manuscripts.
vocabulary.example: With all the grown-ups hard at work in the Scriptorium, there was no one to tell him to go to bed.

vocabulary.definition: Shake or tremble suddenly, typically as a result of fear or revulsion.
vocabulary.example:
”

“Her own words to the Angel of Caprona ,” Aunt Maria said, and shuddered as she picked up her greasy cup.

vocabulary.definition: Done without help from anyone else.
vocabulary.example: Well after midnight, when Giorgio was holding a pass single-handed against the enemy, Tonino happened to hear his father and mother coming to bed.

vocabulary.definition: A form of words supposed to have magical power.
vocabulary.example: And the beauty of it, he thought, eating breakfast without noticing, was that Giorgio had saved his country, not only single-handed, but without a spell coming into it anywhere.

vocabulary.definition: To be silent and bad-tempered out of annoyance or disappointment.
vocabulary.example: Around Tonino, everyone else was complaining and Lucia was sulking.

vocabulary.definition: A melody, especially one that characterizes a certain piece of music.
vocabulary.example: “That’s the strongest tune there is.
